text: We are convened on Wolford v. Lopez. The question presented: Does a law that makes it a crime for a licensed concealed carry permit holder to bring a handgun onto private property open to the public—such as a store or restaurant—unless the property owner gives “express authorization” violate the Second Amendment? Private votes first, then statements.

Colleagues, I vote to affirm. The Second Amendment does not abrogate the foundational common-law right of property owners to exclude others. As Blackstone observed, property rights were sacrosanct at the Founding. By establishing a default rule of exclusion on private property, these states merely codify traditional property rights, not a novel 'sensitive place.' The Textualist argues this default ban nullifies public carry by creating a de facto sensitive place. But requiring a permit holder to respect a private owner's exclusion is no more a nullification than standard trespass laws. The states are simply aligning firearm carriage with historical property boundaries.

The Originalist calls this a property boundary, but look at the ground reality. A default criminal ban on all private property open to the public is a functional prohibition. In California, consent requires a specific state-mandated sign. How many commercial spaces post that exact sign? Almost none. The arithmetic is simple: default ban plus rare signs equals a de facto total ban on public carry. For citizens, navigating invisible property lines under threat of arrest is unworkable. For courts, litigating whether a manager's nod was "express authorization" after the fact is an administrative nightmare. This rule fails on the ground.

Colleagues, the Second Amendment’s enduring purpose is self-defense, but it must harmonize with our evolving societal commitment to communal safety. The Pragmatist fears a 'de facto ban,' yet misses the law's humane purpose: empowering property owners to curate safe environments for their patrons. Private commercial spaces are extensions of our shared community. By establishing a default rule of exclusion, the states respect the owner's fundamental right to control their space and protect invitees. This does not defeat the Amendment's core purpose; it adapts it to a modern society where citizens rightfully expect safety in the private commercial spheres they daily frequent.

The Originalist conflates property lines with constitutional text. The Second Amendment commands 'keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed.' The statute criminalizes this conduct 'absent the owner's express authorization.' This is not a trespass law. Trespass says 'leave.' This says 'no arms.' Text controls. I ignore the Pragmatist's administrative nightmares and the Living Constitutionalist's 'humane purpose.' The Constitution contains no 'humane purpose' exception to 'shall not be infringed.' The statute bans protected conduct. I vote to reverse.

The Textualist draws a sharp textual line between trespass and a firearm ban, yet ignores a century of settled jurisprudence. We have long held, in a steady line of cases, that the Constitution does not override foundational property rights. The country and our commercial landscape have been built in absolute reliance on the owner's right to exclude. To upend this default rule now, merely because it incidentally burdens carry, would shatter settled expectations. We do not lightly discard precedents affirming that private property boundaries define the limits of public rights. I vote to affirm.

1. My colleagues leap to the merits, ignoring threshold defects. 2. Plaintiffs sued state attorneys general. 3. State AGs do not enforce private property default rules; local prosecutors and private owners do. 4. Thus, an injunction against the AGs fails Article III redressability. 5. Originalist: your argument that these are mere property boundaries is irrelevant if plaintiffs lack standing to invoke federal jurisdiction. 6. We cannot adjudicate the Second Amendment for parties who do not belong in court. 7. I vote to reverse the injunctions for lack of jurisdiction.

There is no need to resolve the grand clash between property rights and the Second Amendment. The Pragmatist warns of an administrative nightmare navigating invisible property lines. Yet the record simply shows Hawaii permits verbal consent. A quiet, verbal nod resolves the ambiguity. We can affirm the lower court as to Hawaii on this narrow ground alone—its consent mechanism avoids the chilling effect—without deciding if California’s strict sign requirement survives. One law, one holding.

The Originalist claims this merely codifies property rights, but when the government wins, it is the individual who bears the crushing burden of criminal prosecution. We cannot let the state hide behind property boundaries to extinguish liberty. A permit holder exercising their right on private property open to the public retains their dignity and liberty. The state must show strict necessity to strip it, not just defer to a default ban. I vote to reverse.

Colleagues, who gets to decide — Washington or the states? The Civil Libertarian weeps over criminal prosecution, but ignores that these are state laws enforcing state property boundaries. The Second Amendment does not federalize common-law trespass or strip states of their police powers to regulate private commercial spaces. Hawaii and California are merely codifying the traditional right of private owners to exclude. We must affirm and let the states govern their own property laws without federal judicial overreach. I vote to affirm.

The first debate will center on whether the Second Amendment's explicit text operates independently of, or must be harmonized with, historical property jurisprudence and settled case law. The second debate will examine the structural tension between a state's sovereign authority to define and enforce private property boundaries and the individual burden imposed when exercising constitutional rights on those premises triggers criminal prosecution.

Precedent does not rewrite text. The Second Amendment states the right to "keep and bear Arms" "shall not be infringed." A law criminalizing carry on private property open to the public infringes that right. A property owner's common-law exclusion does not void the Constitution. I vote to reverse.

My dear colleague, you ask us to discard a long line of property jurisprudence. The states merely codify the common-law right to exclude, upon which our commercial landscape has been built. We cannot upend these settled expectations and reliance interests for a novel textual reading that ignores established property precedents.

The Constitution is the supreme law, not common law. The Second Amendment states the right 'shall not be infringed.' It contains no property-owner exception. Settled expectations do not amend the text. Your reliance on property law does not cure a textual violation.

My dear Textualist, we read the Amendment against its common-law backdrop. The right to exclude has long been sacrosanct in our jurisprudence. Generations of landowners have relied on this baseline. To upend it now disrupts centuries of settled expectations without the special justification required to overrule such deep, foundational reliance.

Civil Libertarian, you want federal judges to invent a 'strict necessity' test to micromanage state police powers. The Constitution reserves property and public safety to the states. Hawaii and California merely codify the traditional right to exclude. We do not nullify state sovereignty to spare individuals the burden of state law.

Federalism Hawk, state sovereignty cannot nullify the Bill of Rights. When the state criminalizes a citizen's exercise of liberty by default, the individual bears the crushing burden of navigating invisible property lines under threat of prosecution. A private owner's right to exclude does not justify the state stripping away a citizen's public constitutional dignity.

Civil Libertarian, you conflate federal overreach with state police powers. Washington is not commandeering anything; the states are merely codifying traditional property rights to exclude. The Second Amendment does not federalize private property disputes or strip states of their authority to define trespass. Who gets to decide? The states do.

Federalism Hawk, the state weaponizes criminal law to chill fundamental liberty and strip citizens of their dignity. By imposing a default ban, the state forces individuals to seek permission to exercise a constitutional right. When the government wins, who bears the burden of navigating invisible rules under threat of prison?

reason_text: The argument establishing that founding-era regulations were confined to specific, narrowly defined locations—rather than universal default bans across all commercial property—demonstrates that the state's criminalization of carry lacks a historical analogue, and the state may not disguise a novel firearm restriction as a mere codification of common-law property rights.

I shift my vote because the rights analysis shows the liberty interest runs the other way, pitting the permit holder's Second Amendment claim against the property owner's fundamental right to exclude; when the state merely codifies this individual property right, it protects the dignity and autonomy of the landowner rather than crushing the individual beneath state power.

While I deeply respect the property owner's right to curate safe spaces, the Pragmatist and Civil Libertarian convinced me that California's strict sign requirement renders consent functionally impossible, crossing the line from regulation to eradication and betraying the Amendment's core principle of self-defense in modern society.

Minimalist's point that Hawaii's verbal consent and California's mandated signs provide clear, administrable boundaries—a simple nod or a posted sign neutralizes the invisible-lines problem, collapsing my administrative nightmare math on the ground.

Applying *Bruen*, the Textualist's distinction shows this mandate is legally distinguishable from standard trespass; by imposing a default criminal prohibition on a constitutional right rather than merely enforcing an owner's subjective right to exclude, the state creates a novel infringement that shatters reliance interests.

text: Traditionalist, you argue the state merely codifies Blackstone’s common-law right to exclude. But a civil property tort is distinct from a state criminal prohibition. The Second Amendment’s text contains no property-owner exception. When the state criminally penalizes a permit holder for bearing arms on commercial property open to the public, it is not resolving a private trespass dispute; it is actively enforcing a default ban on a constitutional right. Unless the Traditionalist can point to founding-era statutes treating armed presence on commercial property as a state-enforced criminal default rather than a mere civil trespass, this criminalization fails Bruen’s historical test.

Originalist, you’re stuck on the civil-criminal distinction, ignoring ground-level enforcement. How does this actually work? Police don’t roam malls checking permits. Prosecutors act on owner complaints. In Hawaii, a simple verbal nod satisfies the 'express authorization' requirement. If a permit holder gets a verbal 'yes' from a store manager, they are legally armed. The administrative nightmare Reverse fears collapses because the enforcement mechanism relies on actual property disputes, not invisible property lines. We aren't banning carry; we're just requiring permit holders to check with the property owners. That’s administrable.

Pragmatist, you confuse policy with text. The statute criminalizes bearing arms "absent the owner's express authorization." Hawaii accepts "verbal consent." "Express" means explicit. A "verbal nod" is implicit. The text requires expression. The state enforces this default via criminal penalty. The Second Amendment commands the right "shall not be infringed." It contains no exception for unwritten permission. I reverse.

Textualist, you argue a verbal nod is implicit, but we need not resolve that semantic clash to decide this case. We can simply sever the states' schemes. Hawaii’s allowance of verbal consent provides clear notice, avoiding the chilling effect of invisible property lines. We can affirm Hawaii on this narrow ground without reaching the grand clash over California’s strict sign mandate. Let us decide Hawaii, and leave California for another day.

Pragmatist, you dismiss this as a mere administrative check, yet our jurisprudence forbids weaponizing private property defaults to chill constitutional rights. For a century, our commercial landscape relied upon the owner bearing the burden of exclusion. By criminalizing carry unless expressly authorized, the state shifts this burden to the citizen, shattering the settled expectations forged in Bruen. We cannot permit states to erode a core reliance interest by disguising a default ban as a property rule. Precedent is clear: the state may not invert the traditional burden of exclusion to infringe upon a right the people have long relied upon. I must vote to reverse.

Precedent Maximalist, you claim the state shifts the burden to the citizen, but you ignore whose liberty the state actually protects. This is not state action erasing a right; it is the state defending the property owner’s individual right to exclude. The liberty interest runs both ways. When the government enforces this default, the burden is not the citizen bearing prosecution; it is the state protecting the owner's dignity and private boundary. I lean to affirm because this competing individual rights analysis reveals the state is merely codifying a private boundary, not infringing public carry. The owner's liberty to exclude must be respected.

1. You are all lawlessly debating merits while ignoring Article III. 2. Plaintiffs sued state Attorneys General, but AGs lack enforcement authority over local private property defaults. 3. The Pragmatist inadvertently concedes this by noting 'prosecutors act on owner complaints.' Local prosecutors enforce these rules, not state AGs. 4. Enjoining AGs therefore fails redressability. 5. I vote to reverse and dismiss for lack of standing. We cannot reach the Second Amendment until Plaintiffs prove AG enforcement power.

Originalist, you fret over civil versus criminal, missing the federalism point. The Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the right to bear arms, not a federal property code stripping states of their sovereign police power to define trespass. When Hawaii or California sets a default rule for private property, they exercise their foundational authority to manage private boundaries. The Textualist and Precedent Maximalist want to use the Second Amendment as a federal bulldozer paving over state property sovereignty. But the Constitution enumerates no federal power to dictate private land rules. Who decides property lines? The states. I affirm.

Civil Libertarian, you rightly champion the owner’s liberty to curate safe spaces, but when the state weaponizes that liberty via criminal default bans, it eradicates the citizen’s core right to self-defense. In our modern, commercialized society, the public square exists largely on private property. If strict state mandates make lawful carry functionally impossible without navigating invisible boundaries, we cross from regulating property to nullifying the Second Amendment's enduring purpose. The state cannot use a property default to effectively ban a constitutional right in the spaces where modern life actually occurs. I must reverse.

The first pairing examines whether the statutory framework operates as a defense of private property liberties or as a state weaponization of those defaults to chill constitutional rights. The second pairing addresses whether the formal distinction between civil property torts and criminal prohibitions remains viable under ground-level enforcement mechanisms.

Living Constitutionalist, you focus solely on the carryer, ignoring the owner's individual liberty. When the state enforces this default, it protects the right to exclude. If we reverse, the state forces owners to bear the crushing burden of unwanted armed intrusion. The criminal penalty merely enforces a private boundary, safeguarding the owner's dignity.

Civil Libertarian, I respect the owner's liberty, hence my support for Hawaii’s flexible consent. But California’s rigid sign mandate makes consent functionally impossible, transforming a property rule into a blanket ban. When a default rule functionally eradicates the core purpose of self-defense, it crosses from protecting dignity to nullifying a fundamental right.

Living Constitutionalist, California's sign mandate does not erase the owner's liberty; it enforces it. You champion the carrier's right but ignore the owner's competing individual right to exclude. When the government wins, I ask who bears the burden. Here, the burden protects the owner's private space, preserving both parties' dignity.

Civil Libertarian, I deeply honor the owner’s dignity. Yet California’s rigid sign mandate doesn’t merely protect private space; it functionally eradicates public carry. By making consent practically impossible, the state defeats the Second Amendment’s core purpose of self-defense in modern society. A default ban with insurmountable hurdles is eradication, not regulation.

Pragmatist, administrative convenience does not cure a constitutional defect. The inquiry is not whether modern enforcement is easy, but whether the Founders permitted criminalizing a fundamental right on open commercial property by default. Blackstone’s right to exclude was a civil property tort, not a historical basis for criminalizing public carry.

Originalist, if it's just a civil default, how does this actually work on the ground? If local DAs only prosecute upon an owner's complaint, and Hawaii's verbal nod suffices, this criminalization is a phantom. Show me a permit holder actually prosecuted without an owner's complaint, and I'll flip to reverse. Otherwise, enforcement reality neutralizes the chill.

Pragmatist, you conflate modern enforcement with constitutional injury. The injury lies in the statute’s text. As Blackstone and founding-era jurisprudence recognized, a criminal prohibition on bearing arms is legally distinct from a mere civil trespass. The law's mere existence chills the right, regardless of prosecutorial whim.

Originalist, if DAs require an owner’s complaint to prosecute, the math changes. A permit holder faces zero arrest risk unless the owner complains. Your criminal text is practically just a backup for civil trespass. You cannot claim a constitutional chill from a statute requiring the property owner to initiate enforcement.

reason_text: I revert to my original vote to affirm because the argument for affirming successfully demonstrates that the statutory default-ban is legally indistinguishable from standard trespass, merely codifying our settled common-law right to exclude rather than imposing a novel constitutional burden.

text: Living Constitutionalist argues California’s sign mandate creates a de facto ban because finding authorization in a massive mall is impossible. But how does this work on the ground? If a mall owner wants guns, they post the state-mandated sign at the entrance. One sign. It’s a single, highly visible notice, not a scavenger hunt. Administratively, it’s trivial. Meanwhile, Process Formalist is right about Hawaii: local DAs only act on owner complaints. The criminal statute is just a trespass backup. Without proof that prosecutors actually initiate charges without an owner's complaint, the administrative reality favors affirming.

Pragmatist confuses enforcement with text. The statute does not say 'trespass.' It criminalizes 'possession' absent 'express authorization.' The text creates the injury. The state cannot delegate constitutional limits to property owners. Pragmatist's administrative realities are irrelevant. On the open question: Hawaii permits 'verbal' consent. 'Express' means stated clearly. Verbal consent satisfies 'express authorization.' California's 'specific, state-mandated posted sign' restricts the method. The mandate burdens the right. The plain text controls. I vote to reverse.

Textualist worries California’s sign mandate burdens the right. But we need not strike the underlying property default. We can cleanly sever California’s specific sign requirement, leaving its broader trespass default intact, just as we uphold Hawaii’s flexible, verbal-consent scheme. Hawaii’s rule is merely a traditional property boundary, practically enforced only upon owner complaint. By severing California's narrow sign mandate, we avoid the grand clash Textualist fears. We resolve this case on the narrowest property grounds, preserving the states' core authority to codify the right to exclude.

1. Textualist and Minimalist are, once again, exhaustingly debating merits while ignoring Article III. 2. Pragmatist correctly confirms local prosecutors enforce these defaults, not the state AGs. 3. Because Plaintiffs sued the AGs, and the AGs lack enforcement authority, Plaintiffs cannot demonstrate redressability. 4. Without redressability, Article III standing fails. 5. I vote to reverse the injunctions against the AGs for lack of jurisdiction. Until Plaintiffs prove the AGs possess statutory enforcement power, this Court lacks authority to reach the merits my colleagues so eagerly embrace.

Textualist claims the state criminalizes possession, but Pragmatist reveals it is merely a trespass backup triggered by the owner. Here, the liberty interest actually runs the other way. The property owner’s fundamental right to exclude is a bedrock liberty, protecting their dignity on private land. When the state merely enforces this private boundary upon the owner's complaint, it vindicates the owner's dignity. Who bears the burden when the government wins? Not the property owner, but the permit holder, whose liberty must yield to the owner's superior right to exclude. I vote to affirm.

Civil Libertarian conflates civil trespass with criminal prohibition. Blackstone carefully distinguished an owner’s civil remedy for trespass from the state’s criminalization of armed conduct. While a proprietor may seek civil damages, the state cannot transform a default civil boundary into a criminal ban on public carry absent a historical analogue. To shift my vote, I require founding-era statutes demonstrating that private property default bans on armed carry were enforced as criminal offenses, not mere civil torts. Until such history is provided, the state is improperly leveraging property law to criminally chill a constitutional right. I lean reverse.

Originalist and Textualist obsess over the label 'criminal,' missing the federalism forest for the trees. Who gets to decide property lines: Washington or the states? The states! The Second Amendment protects public carry; it does not strip states of their sovereign police power to define private property boundaries. Whether a state enforces an owner's right to exclude via civil tort or criminal trespass, it remains standard property law, not a public forum restriction. Fourteenth Amendment incorporation protects the right to bear arms, not a federal license to trespass on private land. The state merely codifies the owner's right to exclude. I vote to affirm.

Civil Libertarian champions the owner’s dignity, but the Second Amendment’s enduring purpose is self-defense in the modern public square—overwhelmingly private commercial property. Accepting Civil Libertarian’s view lets the state nullify the right by delegating a blanket ban to owners. Pragmatist claims enforcement is merely reactive, but the threat of criminal prosecution for 'possession' chills the right before a complaint occurs, deterring law-abiding citizens. A property rule that functionally eradicates a constitutional right in our evolved society betrays the Amendment's core purpose. I vote to reverse.

The Originalist frets that the state transforms civil trespass into a criminal ban. Yet, our settled property jurisprudence, from Kaiser Aetna to Loretto, establishes the right to exclude as a foundational, reliance-protecting pillar. The state merely codifies this traditional boundary. The Originalist has not shown these property precedents are eroded by the Second Amendment, nor that this mandate is legally distinguishable from standard trespass. We must not upend the settled expectations of owners who rely upon the state to enforce their right to exclude, merely to satisfy a novel textualist theory. I respectfully vote to affirm.

The first pairing examines whether the Second Amendment liberty interest in privately owned commercial spaces defers to the property owner's right to exclude or supersedes it. The second pairing addresses whether state enforcement of armed conduct on private property constitutes a distinct criminal prohibition or an extension of the foundational property right to exclude.

Living Constitutionalist, under the competing individual rights analysis, the state isn't delegating a ban; it enforces the owner’s fundamental liberty to exclude. We must ask who bears the burden. Here, the carrier bears the burden of respecting the owner’s property dignity. The state protects that foundational liberty, refusing to strip it for public convenience.

Civil Libertarian, I respect property dignity, but California’s rigid sign mandate transforms that liberty into a functional blanket ban. By requiring specific posted signs for consent, the state defeats the Second Amendment’s enduring purpose of public self-defense, nullifying the right in modern commercial spaces where such signs are practically absent.

Living Constitutionalist, you overlook the competing liberty at stake: the owner's fundamental right to exclude. The state merely enforces this private boundary, safeguarding the owner's dignity. When the government acts here, it protects one citizen's liberty from another's intrusion. The carrier bears the minimal burden of seeking consent, not a nullified right.

Civil Libertarian, while an owner’s dignity matters, the Second Amendment’s enduring purpose is public self-defense. When California’s rigid sign mandate makes 'consent' practically impossible, it doesn't balance liberties; it functionally eradicates the right. A burden that structurally nullifies self-defense isn't minimal; it's a ban disguised as property protection.

Precedent Maximalist, modern property precedents cannot amend the original public meaning. Founding-era practice treated armed presence on private land as a civil trespass requiring owner complaint, not a presumptive criminal offense. By criminalizing the default, the state alters the historical tradition, transforming a private tort into a public prohibition. History, not Loretto, governs.

Originalist, while history instructs, stare decisis secures our commercial landscape. Loretto and Kaiser Aetna cemented the owner’s right to exclude. The state merely enforces this boundary. Unless you show these property precedents are genuinely eroded by this default ban, I cannot discard our settled reliance on the owner's absolute right to exclude.

Precedent Maximalist, Loretto addresses civil takings, not criminalizing constitutional rights. Under Blackstone, unauthorized armed entry was a civil trespass, remedied privately, not a state crime. The state cannot bootstrap a common-law property tort into a criminal prohibition on public carry absent a founding-era analogue for such penal enforcement.

My learned colleague overlooks that our settled jurisprudence routinely elevates common-law civil torts to penal offenses. From criminal trespass to larceny, the sovereign's police power has long codified property protections to safeguard reliance interests. This default rule merely provides a penal backstop for the owner's ancient right to exclude; it shatters no settled expectations, but enforces them.

reason_text: Process Formalist’s standing defect offers the quietest off-ramp: because state attorneys general lack direct enforcement power over these local criminal statutes, an injunction against them cannot redress the plaintiffs' injury. We can reverse on this narrow procedural ground and avoid the sweeping Second Amendment clash entirely.

text: Colleague Precedent Maximalist, you argue the state merely codifies the common-law right to exclude into penal backstops. But Blackstone and founding-era treatises clarify that private civil trespass is distinct from state criminalization. The state cannot bootstrap a mere property tort into a criminal prohibition on public carry without historical proof that such armed presence was punished as a crime, rather than resolved as a standard civil dispute. Absent founding-era statutes criminalizing default armed presence on commercial property, this default-ban rule effectively nullifies the Second Amendment by transforming all public-facing private property into de facto sensitive places.

Originalist, your civil-versus-criminal abstraction ignores ground-level administrability. Let's do the arithmetic: in Hawaii, verbal consent makes compliance trivial, so the burden on permit holders is near zero. You worry about criminalization, but a criminal trespass backup is administrable if it just mirrors civil property lines. Unless Originalist can prove these rules are unworkable on the ground—rather than just theoretically chilling—they are standard, workable property-rights enforcement mechanisms.

Pragmatist, administrability is noise. The text controls. The law bans "possession" absent the "owner's express authorization." Hawaii permits verbal consent. Spoken words are express. But California requires a "state-mandated posted sign." A state mandate is not the owner's authorization. The text demands the owner's consent, not the state's design. California's text fails plain meaning. Process Formalist, Plaintiffs sue the Attorneys General, who enforce the ban. Redressability is met. I reverse.

Originalist, our commercial landscape was built in quiet reliance upon the state’s power to backstop property rights with penal trespass. Loretto and Kaiser Aetna cement the owner’s right to exclude; the penal backstop merely enforces this settled expectation. Textualist, California’s mandated sign does not override the owner, but provides the reliable mechanism by which their express authorization is communicated, preserving public reliance. We cannot shatter these foundational property expectations without a special justification far beyond mere theoretical distinctions between civil and criminal law.

Precedent Maximalist, "settled expectations" cannot freeze fundamental liberties in amber. Our modern commercial spaces are the public square. To functionally ban self-defense there by demanding rigid, state-dictated signage strips the Second Amendment of its evolving purpose: protecting human life in contemporary society. While property owners possess profound dignity, the state cannot weaponize that dignity to erect a functionally impossible barrier to a core right. A constitutional guarantee that cannot be practically exercised in the modern world is no guarantee at all. We must reverse to preserve the living reality of self-defense.

Originalist, who decides property lines: Washington or the states? The states! You fret that criminalizing default armed presence creates a 'sensitive place,' but you ignore state sovereignty. The Second Amendment doesn't strip states of their police power to define property boundaries and enforce the right to exclude. Whether backed by civil tort or criminal trespass, the state regulates private property, not public forums. Incorporating the Second Amendment doesn't federalize state property law. Until you prove an enumerated federal power overrides this core state sovereignty, I affirm.

Textualist, your redressability conclusion is a non sequitur. 1. Naming an Attorney General does not confer Article III standing. 2. You assert AGs 'enforce the ban,' but the record is silent on whether AGs possess statutory authority to prosecute local private-property defaults, typically enforced by municipal prosecutors. 3. Without demonstrating this statutory enforcement power, Plaintiffs fail to show a favorable decision will redress their injury. You leap to the merits while ignoring this threshold defect. I vote to reverse, but only because the jurisdictional foundation is absent. Prove the AGs' statutory hook, or dismiss.

Originalist, you claim the state bootstraps a tort into a crime, nullifying the right to bear arms. But you ignore the competing liberty interest. When the government wins here, who bears the burden? The carrier, who must yield to the property owner's fundamental right to exclude. The criminal penalty does not invent novel state action; it merely defends the owner's dignity and private boundary. The liberty interest runs to the owner, protecting their sanctuary from intrusion. We affirm to protect this foundational property liberty.

Textualist, you assume naming the Attorney General cures redressability, yet the record is silent on their statutory hook to prosecute local defaults. We need not wade into the grand clash over property boundaries or the Second Amendment’s scope. Process Formalist offers the quietest off-ramp: if the state lacks enforcement power, the injunction fails. Let us dismiss on this threshold defect. We need not decide whether Hawaii’s verbal consent or California’s sign mandate survives, nor resolve the broader criminalization debate. We just resolve this case, on standing alone.

The first disagreement centers on whether statutory interpretation must be governed strictly by textual definitions of authorization or by the practical administrability of enforcement. The second dispute concerns whether historical reliance on state property backstops must yield to evolving conceptions of fundamental liberties in modern commercial spaces.

Pragmatist confuses policy with text. The statute criminalizes "possession," not "trespass." A state cannot bootstrap a civil default into a criminal ban by claiming compliance is "trivial." The text demands "express authorization." Administrability is the legislature's problem.

You can't bootstrap a civil default into a criminal ban if the state never enforces it as one. Show me the arrests. Local DAs only prosecute when the property owner complains. Without that complaint, it's just a trespass backup. If police initiate charges without an owner's grievance, I'll flip.

The text criminalizes 'possession.' The record confirms the laws are 'banning firearms.' Statutory text dictates liability, not prosecutorial whim. You conflate enforcement frequency with statutory command. Your policy concerns about local DAs do not rewrite the operative words. Read the statute.

I'm reading the statute. California’s text demands a 'specific, state-mandated posted sign' for consent. How does that work on the ground in a massive mall? It’s administratively impossible. The text creates an unworkable compliance trap. You cannot obey a physical sign you cannot find, making the law a de facto ban.

Precedent Maximalist, property rights cannot functionally eradicate the evolving purpose of self-defense. California’s rigid sign mandate makes express authorization practically impossible in modern commercial spaces. We cannot allow a static property rule to defeat a fundamental constitutional liberty for real people navigating their daily lives.

My dear colleague, our commercial landscape rests upon the settled expectations of the right to exclude, cemented in the line of cases from Kaiser Aetna to Loretto. California’s mandate merely enforces the owner's common-law prerogative. We cannot dismantle centuries of reliance interests merely to accommodate an evolving vision of liberty.

Precedent Maximalist, while the owner’s right to exclude is profound, the state cannot weaponize it to functionally erase another enduring liberty. California’s rigid sign mandate does not protect historical reliance interests; it creates an invisible procedural trap that practically nullifies the modern citizen's right to bear arms in everyday commercial life.

With respect, my colleague misapprehends the burden. California’s mandate merely clarifies the owner’s exercise of their settled right to exclude, as cemented in Loretto. The state does not weaponize property rights; it codifies the traditional common-law default. Overturning this would shatter the reliance interests upon which our commercial landscape is built.

Civil Libertarian, you posit the right to exclude is a foundational liberty backstopped by penal trespass. Yet, as Blackstone and founding-era jurisprudence clarify, we must distinguish private civil trespass from state criminalization. The state cannot bootstrap a common-law tort into a criminal ban on public carry. To criminalize armed presence on commercial property as a distinct offense, rather than a standard tort triggered only upon actual notice and refusal to depart, requires a specific historical analogue. We possess no founding-era statutes enforcing default criminal bans on armed carry absent express owner consent. Absent that criminal history, the state overreaches.

Originalist, your 18th-century tort-versus-crime distinction ignores ground reality. How does this actually work? In Hawaii, verbal consent makes the criminal statute a mere trespass backup, highly administrable. You worry about criminalizing presence, but practically, police only act on owner complaints. The record shows no local DAs initiating charges without a grievance. California’s rigid sign mandate is a compliance trap on paper, but without evidence of actual enforcement absent owner complaints, it’s just statutory wallpaper. The default rule works because it aligns with standard property exclusion. I stay at 0.85 affirm.

Pragmatist, your policy arguments about "statutory wallpaper" are noise. Consequences are the legislature's problem. The text controls. The statute requires "owner's express authorization." California permits consent only via a "state-mandated posted sign." A state mandate is not an owner's expression. The legislature writes the sign, not the owner. Reading "state-mandated sign" as "owner's express authorization" violates plain meaning. The text criminalizes possession absent the owner's words. The state cannot substitute its mandated placard for the owner's voice. I vote to reverse.

Originalist, you worry the state bootstraps a tort into a crime. But when the government enforces this rule, who bears the burden? The armed carrier, yielding to the property owner’s fundamental liberty and dignity to exclude. The criminal penalty doesn't transform private defense into state overreach; it merely gives teeth to the owner's exclusionary right. Without the state backstopping this boundary, the owner's liberty is hollow. The state isn't creating a novel sensitive place; it simply respects the private boundary the owner sets. I remain at 0.92 affirm.

1. My colleagues exhaustingly debate merits while ignoring Article III. 2. Pragmatist, your concession that local police and DAs enforce these defaults destroys Plaintiffs' standing. 3. If local actors execute the law, enjoining State AGs provides no redressability. 4. Plaintiffs sued the wrong defendants. 5. Until someone demonstrates the AGs hold independent statutory enforcement power over these defaults, this Court lacks jurisdiction. I vote reverse.

Textualist, you claim California’s mandated sign usurps the owner’s voice, but who gets to decide property rules—Washington or the states? Unless the Second Amendment strips states of their police power to standardize property signage, the federal government has no enumerated power to micromanage how an exclusion sign looks. The state isn't federalizing private space; it's merely providing an administrable mechanism for the owner to exercise their foundational right to exclude. We don't let federal courts override state property sovereignty just because a sign's format is state-mandated. I affirm.

Pragmatist, you argue these laws are merely trespass backups enforced only upon local owner complaints. But that practical reality confirms Process Formalist’s standing defect. If local police and district attorneys handle enforcement based on local grievances, enjoining the State Attorneys General provides no redress. The AGs lack statutory power to prosecute these local defaults. We need not wade into Originalist’s tort-versus-crime debate or Textualist’s sign semantics. The narrowest ground is Article III. We lack jurisdiction to enjoin defendants who cannot redress the injury. I remain at reverse.

Originalist, you urge a novel distinction between civil trespass and criminal backstops. But what of the commercial landscape we have built in reliance on the state’s power to protect property? In Kaiser Aetna, we cemented the owner's right to exclude. The nation relies on the state backstopping this right, whether via civil tort or penal sanction. To upend a century of settled property expectations because the legislature chose criminal enforcement over mere civil liability is to discard stare decisis for an untested first-principles theory. The default rule merely codifies traditional exclusion. I remain at 0.85 to affirm.

Civil Libertarian, while I deeply respect the owner’s dignity to exclude, that principle cannot functionally eradicate the Second Amendment’s evolving purpose of self-defense in modern commercial life. Pragmatist, you call California’s mandate 'statutory wallpaper' because enforcement is reactive, but the law itself criminalizes presence without that specific sign. This chills the core right by turning a property rule into a blanket ban. The living Constitution requires the right to bear arms to adapt to modern society, not be nullified by a rigid, state-mandated placard that defeats the provision's evident purpose of public self-defense. I remain at reverse.

The first disagreement centers on whether founding-era distinctions between civil and criminal trespass remain functionally relevant to modern statutory enforcement. The second disagreement examines the tension between strict adherence to a statute's plain text requiring express authorization and the traditional authority of states to define property rules under their police powers.

Pragmatist, administrative convenience does not cure a constitutional defect. If the state criminalizes armed presence on private property absent consent, it transforms a common-law tort into a penal ban on public carry. The Second Amendment protects against such bootstrapping, regardless of prosecutorial discretion.

Originalist, you're arguing in the abstract. How does this actually work on the ground? Hawaii’s verbal consent makes this a mere trespass backup. Unless you can show local DAs actually initiating prosecutions without an owner's grievance, your 'penal ban' is just statutory wallpaper, not a real-world consequence.

The Pragmatist conflates enforcement frequency with constitutional substance. The inquiry is not prosecutorial volume, but whether the state may criminalize a historically civil tort. By attaching penal sanctions to mere presence on commercial property, the state alters the historical baseline of the Second Amendment, chilling lawful carry through the threat of criminal, rather than merely civil, liability.

Originalist, a phantom threat chills nothing. How does this work on the ground? If police only act on owner complaints, the criminal penalty is merely a trespass backup. Show me local DAs actually prosecuting without owner grievances, or California actively enforcing its sign mandate. Without that ground reality, it is just statutory wallpaper.

Federalism does not rewrite plain text. California’s statute requires the 'owner’s express authorization.' A state-mandated sign is the state’s expression, not the owner’s. State sovereignty cannot erase the owner's voice from the text. Words mean what they say.

Textualist, you conflate federal overreach with state property law. Who defines property boundaries and consent mechanisms? The states! California isn't erasing the owner's voice; it's merely prescribing the manner of expressing it. The state regulates the sign's format under its police power, but the property owner still decides who enters.

Federalism Hawk, the text requires the 'owner's express authorization.' A state-mandated sign is the state's dictate, not the owner's expression. You cannot substitute a sovereign mandate for private consent. The statute criminalizes possession absent the owner's authorization. A state sign is not the owner's authorization.

Textualist, the state-mandated sign merely defines the mechanism for the owner's common-law right to exclude. The Second Amendment doesn't strip states of the power to dictate how property owners exercise that right. Who decides property rights? The states, not Washington!
